target.title: fix(gateway): skip health-monitor restart for terminal WhatsApp disconnects
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: On multi-tenant gateways running ≥1 WhatsApp account, any account that receives a terminal disconnect (`loggedOut` 401, `connectionReplaced` 440) enters a perpetual restart loop — the health monitor calls `startChannel()` on every check interval, each attempt leaks a full Baileys WebSocket + Noise handshake, and over time this exhausts gateway heap (12.9 GB reported over 20 h on 12 accounts), saturates the event loop (~80% CPU), and degrades RPC latency for all tenants on the same gateway (~24 s p99 vs. <200 ms baseline). Recovery requires manual intervention.

- **Root Cause**: Two independent restart paths both lacked a terminal-disconnect guard, and the status surface also missed the signal. (1) `ChannelHealthSnapshot` had no `terminalDisconnect` field, so `evaluateChannelHealth` returned `"not-running"` for every stopped channel regardless of why it stopped, causing the health-monitor to unconditionally call `startChannel()`. (2) The `ChannelManager` task-exit recovery handler fires immediately when the channel task settles — before the health monitor is involved — and only checked `manuallyStopped`, so terminal exits still triggered the backoff-and-restart loop independently. (3) WhatsApp's custom `buildAccountSnapshot` handler forwarded `healthState` from the runtime snapshot but not `terminalDisconnect`, so `channels.status` recomputed `"not-running"` instead of `"terminal-disconnect"` for logged-out accounts.

- **Fix**: Add `terminalDisconnect?: boolean` to `ChannelHealthSnapshot` and the public `ChannelAccountSnapshot`. The WhatsApp status controller sets the flag to `true` in `markStopped()` when `healthState` is `"logged-out"` or `"conflict"` (the only two states that clear auth and require QR re-pairing), and resets it to `undefined` in `noteConnected()` on successful re-auth. Both restart paths now observe the flag: `evaluateChannelHealth` returns a new `"terminal-disconnect"` reason that the health monitor logs and skips; the `ChannelManager` task-exit handler returns early with an info-level log. The flag is also forwarded in WhatsApp's `buildAccountSnapshot` so `channels.status` reflects the correct terminal state. The flag is channel-agnostic: any future extension can set it on its own `ChannelAccountSnapshot` to opt in to the same no-restart behavior without modifying core.

- **What changed**:
  - `src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts` — added `terminalDisconnect?: boolean` to `ChannelAccountSnapshot`
  - `src/gateway/channel-health-policy.ts` — added field to `ChannelHealthSnapshot`; added `"terminal-disconnect"` reason; added guard before the `not-running` branch
  - `src/gateway/channel-health-monitor.ts` — added skip block for `terminal-disconnect` reason with an info-level log line
  - `src/gateway/server-channels.ts` — added `terminalDisconnect` guard in the task-exit recovery handler after the `manuallyStopped` check
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/types.ts` — added `terminalDisconnect?: boolean` to `WebChannelStatus`
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor-state.ts` — set flag in `markStopped()` for `"logged-out"` / `"conflict"`; clear in `noteConnected()`
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/channel.ts` — forwarded `terminalDisconnect` from the runtime snapshot in the custom `buildAccountSnapshot` handler
  - `src/gateway/channel-health-policy.test.ts` — 3 new cases covering terminal-disconnect evaluation, no-flag fallthrough, and running-channel flag ignore
  - `src/gateway/channel-health-monitor.test.ts` — 2 new cases proving the monitor skips terminal-disconnect channels and still restarts non-terminal stopped channels
  - `src/gateway/server-channels.test.ts` — 1 new case proving the manager task-exit path does not auto-restart a terminal-disconnect channel
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/channels.status.test.ts` — 1 new case proving `channels.status` reports `"terminal-disconnect"` health state when the flag is set

- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - No changes to reconnect policy, cooldown config, or hourly restart cap
  - No changes to any other channel extension (Telegram, Discord, Slack, etc.)
  - `isManuallyStopped()` logic is untouched; manually stopped channels continue to be skipped by the existing guard that runs before health evaluation
  - `resolveChannelRestartReason` is not reachable for `terminal-disconnect` (monitor skips before calling it) and is not modified
  - No new public-facing config keys or gateway events in this change

---

### Reproduction

1. Run a gateway with ≥1 WhatsApp account:
   ```yaml
   channels:
     whatsapp:
       accounts:
         default:
           enabled: true
   ```
2. From the linked phone: Settings → Linked Devices → unlink the session.
3. Observe `gateway.log`:
   ```
   [health-monitor] [whatsapp:default] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stopped)
   ```
   firing every check interval indefinitely.
4. After this fix:
   ```
   [default] auto-restart skipped, terminal disconnect
   [health-monitor] [whatsapp:default] health-monitor: skipping restart, terminal disconnect
   ```
   fires with no `startChannel()` call, and `channels.status` reports `healthState: "terminal-disconnect"`.

---

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: A terminal-disconnect channel will no longer be auto-recovered by either restart path. If `terminalDisconnect` is accidentally set `true` on a channel that could have reconnected, that channel would stay stopped until manually restarted.
- **Mitigation**: The flag is set only for `"logged-out"` and `"conflict"` healthStates, which both clear stored auth credentials — the channel genuinely cannot reconnect without operator QR action. `noteConnected()` unconditionally clears the flag, so a successful re-auth restores normal behavior on all paths. Three policy unit tests, two monitor integration tests, one manager recovery test, and one `channels.status` regression test cover the boundary cases.

---

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

---

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway
- [x] WhatsApp plugin
- [x] Channel status types (additive, backward-compatible)
- [x] Tests

---

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #78419

target.body:
## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native subagent runs.
- Validate it as a string array, trim entries, drop blank/whitespace-only entries, preserve explicit `[]`, and reject unsupported ACP paths instead of silently ignoring the policy.
- Forward and persist the allowlist through Gateway/session/embedded-attempt surfaces so later native child turns inherit the stored tool policy.
- Normalize Gateway `agent.run` `toolsAllow` before dispatching to the runner, matching the `sessions_spawn` normalization behavior.
- Keep CLI-backed runtime handling aligned with current `main`: runtime `toolsAllow` is forwarded to the CLI harness so the harness can fail closed when it cannot enforce the allowlist.

## Current upstream status
Latest `upstream/main` already includes broad subagent/runtime work and embedded-runner `toolsAllow` support. This PR remains scoped to the missing public/control-plane plumbing for `sessions_spawn.toolsAllow`, plus Gateway normalization for raw `agent.run` inputs.

## Change Type
- [x] Feature

## Scope
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native subagent tools with `toolsAllow`; the policy is applied to the spawned child embedded runtime so the child receives only allowed tool schemas. Gateway `agent.run` also normalizes direct `toolsAllow` input before runner dispatch.

Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw source checkout at current PR head `230b54baa3175fafeb7160922f510b489a6d1887`, temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, local Gateway on loopback token auth with `gateway.mode=local`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json` through Gateway (not `--local`), and a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server that records every `/v1/responses` request tool schema.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started a local mock OpenAI Responses server from `/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs/mock-openai-toolsallow.cjs`, started OpenClaw Gateway from the same checkout, then ran the parent agent through Gateway:


Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from the current-head Gateway proof summarizer. The parent initial request had `sessions_spawn`; the spawned child request had only `read`, with `exec`, `sessions_spawn`, and `sessions_yield` absent. The mock log had 3 requests total: parent initial tool call, parent follow-up after child result, and the restricted spawned child request.

Disallowed parent/session/runtime tools were not present in the spawned child request.

What was not tested: A paid external provider was not used. The proof used a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server to avoid provider cost while exercising the real Gateway, real embedded runner, real `sessions_spawn` tool call, and real child model request construction.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test

- Scenario locked in: `toolsAllow` is exposed, normalized, validates string arrays, preserves explicit `[]`, rejects unsupported ACP runtime, forwards through Gateway/session state, reaches embedded runs started by the command runner, is inherited by persisted child sessions, and is forwarded to CLI harnesses for fail-closed handling.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`runtime="subagent"` callers may pass `toolsAllow` to narrow a spawned native subagent's tool set. Defaults are unchanged when omitted. `toolsAllow: []` intentionally runs the child without tools. `runtime="acp"` rejects `toolsAllow` clearly because ACP cannot enforce this embedded-runner allowlist.

## Security Impact
- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

Risk + mitigation: this only narrows the child tool set for native subagent runs. Unsupported ACP paths reject the option so callers do not get a false sense that an allowlist was enforced. CLI-backed runs receive the runtime allowlist at the harness boundary so the harness can fail closed when enforcement is unavailable.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: callers may expect `toolsAllow` to affect ACP runtime spawns.
  - Mitigation: the tool rejects `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` with a clear input error.
- Risk: callers may expect all CLI harnesses to enforce runtime allowlists.
  - Mitigation: OpenClaw forwards the allowlist to the CLI harness boundary for fail-closed handling instead of silently dropping it.
- Risk: public API naming/semantics need maintainer judgment.
  - Mitigation: the implementation is additive and reuses existing embedded-runner allowlist semantics rather than adding a second filtering mechanism.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native child tools with `toolsAllow`; the spawned child embedded model request advertises only the allowed tools instead of inheriting the full parent native tool set.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout at PR head `99c0bdd3cb556a7a407c0ae45e0931fa82617dc3`; temporary OpenClaw state/config; real Gateway loopback token auth; real `node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run`; real `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json`; deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server logging `/v1/responses` requests.

target.title: fix: Node allowlist writeback can restore revoked exec approvals
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes a race where execution-side allowlist metadata writeback could save a stale `exec-approvals.json` snapshot after an admin revoked or changed policy.
- Adds a hash/CAS guard plus a file-lock-backed write path for exec approval updates that can race with admin writes.
- Propagates the resolved approvals hash through node-host and gateway execution paths so usage metadata and allow-always persistence only write when the evaluated snapshot is still current.
- Adds regression coverage for stale writeback and the remaining interleaving where the file changes between reload and save.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #78225
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

Execution-time approval helpers updated allowlist usage metadata by mutating and saving an approvals object derived from an earlier policy-evaluation snapshot. Unlike admin approval writes, those metadata updates did not verify the file hash before saving. A write-scoped execution path could therefore overwrite a newer admin policy change with stale allowlist data.

## Regression Test Plan

- Added/updated `src/infra/exec-approvals-store.test.ts` coverage for stale allowlist metadata writeback.
- Covered the race where an admin revocation lands after the execution helper reloads but before it saves.
- Verified the touched execution and admin write paths still typecheck and lint cleanly.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Execution-side allowlist metadata writes are now best-effort when the approvals file changed after policy evaluation. If an admin update wins the race, the metadata write is skipped rather than restoring stale policy.
- Admin approval writes continue to use base-hash validation and now go through the shared locked save helper.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The changed execution path now refuses stale exec-approval writeback instead of broadening command execution. Regression tests cover stale snapshot writeback, and validation passed on the touched files.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw worktree
- Model/provider: github-copilot/gpt-5.4 generated the broader fix; manual recovery added the missing hash helper and reran validation.
- Relevant config: staged high-security submission workflow with release/drift skips recorded for recovery.

### Steps

1. Reproduce the linked issue by evaluating a command against an allowlisted snapshot, revoking that approval via admin policy update, then allowing execution-side metadata writeback to run.
2. Apply this branch.
3. Run the validation commands below.

### Expected

- Execution-side metadata writeback does not restore a revoked or changed approval when the underlying approvals file changed after policy evaluation.
- Validation passes for the touched behavior.

### Actual

- Validation status: passed.

## Evidence

Validation commands run on the amended branch:

- `pnpm exec oxlint src/infra/exec-approvals.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-store.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.ts src/cli/exec-approvals-cli.ts src/cli/exec-policy-cli.ts src/gateway/server-methods/exec-approvals.ts src/node-host/invoke.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm check`
- `pnpm test src/infra/exec-approvals-store.test.ts`

Results:

- Changed-file lint: passed, 0 warnings/errors.
- Build: passed.
- Check/type/lint policy suite: passed.
- Targeted regression test: 19 passed in `src/infra/exec-approvals-store.test.ts`.

CVSS from linked issue bundle:

- CVSS v3.1: 7.1 High
- CVSS v4.0: 7.1 High

Changed files:

- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.ts`
- `src/cli/exec-approvals-cli.ts`
- `src/cli/exec-policy-cli.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-methods/exec-approvals.ts`
- `src/infra/exec-approvals-store.test.ts`
- `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts`
- `src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts`
- `src/node-host/invoke.ts`

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: stale allowlist metadata writeback no longer restores policy when the approvals file hash changed; the remaining reload-before-save race is covered by regression tests.
- Edge cases checked: empty/no-op metadata update, current hash mismatch, and changed-file lint/type/build behavior.
- What you did **not** verify: A full repo-wide `pnpm test` completed previously with baseline/no-output infrastructure noise in the submission workflow; after the final amended commit, verification was limited to targeted regression tests plus build/check/lint.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Approval metadata such as `lastUsedAt` may be skipped when an admin concurrently changes policy.
  - Mitigation: This is intentional fail-closed behavior for policy freshness. A later execution can record metadata once it evaluates the latest approvals snapshot.
- Risk: Locking approval writes changes local write sequencing.
  - Mitigation: The lock is scoped to exec approvals writes and validation passed through build, check, lint, and targeted regression tests.

target.title: fix(memory-core): exclude session-corpus files from short-term promotion (#77831)
target.body:
## Summary
- Dreaming writes session transcripts to `memory/.dreams/session-corpus/` inside the memory tree. The short-term promotion system tracked these paths in the recall store correctly (so dreaming phases can measure organic recall frequency), but also allowed them to pass through both promotion gates — causing 83% of recall-store entries to become session-corpus noise that polluted `MEMORY.md` with raw JSON, REM phase markers, and evidence lines.
- Added `isSessionCorpusPath()` helper that matches `memory/.dreams/session-corpus/*.txt|md` paths.
- Applied the guard in two places: (1) `recordShortTermRecalls` — corpus snippets are now skipped before entering the recall store as promotion candidates; (2) `rankShortTermPromotionCandidates` — existing corpus entries are skipped during candidate ranking.
- `isShortTermMemoryPath()` is unchanged so dreaming-phase signal tracking continues to work correctly.

Closes #77831

## Testing
```
pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts
```
```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  47 passed (47)
   Start at  19:36:25
   Duration  1.56s
```

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior: `rankShortTermPromotionCandidates` returned `memory/.dreams/session-corpus/YYYY-MM-DD.txt` entries as promotion candidates despite those files being internal corpus data; the new `isSessionCorpusPath()` guard excludes them while keeping daily memory notes eligible.
- Tested via targeted vitest test added in this PR. No live OpenClaw runtime available — please apply maintainer `proof:` override or advise on evidence format.

target.title: fix(memory-core): preserve sibling supplement results when one search rejects (#77897)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #77897. `searchMemoryCorpusSupplements` (extensions/memory-core/src/tools.shared.ts) used `Promise.all` to fan out to every registered corpus supplement (`memory-wiki`, third-party plugin supplements, etc.). `Promise.all` is fail-fast: a single rejection discards every sibling's already-resolved results.

This patch swaps `Promise.all` → `Promise.allSettled`, then re-flattens the fulfilled values. Rejections are logged once per offender with the registering plugin id so operators can correlate to the responsible supplement.

## Behavior contract (mathematical guarantee)

Let `S` be the set of registered corpus supplements at call time. Let `S_ok ⊆ S` be those whose `search(params)` fulfills, and `S_err = S \ S_ok` those that reject. The function now satisfies:

```
result = sort_score_desc_then_path(⋃_{s ∈ S_ok} s.search(params)).slice(0, max(1, maxResults ?? 10))
```

In particular:
- `|S_err| ≥ 1` does not collapse `result` to `[]`. Sibling results survive.
- `|S_err| = |S|` returns `[]` (graceful degradation, not throw).
- `|S_err| = 0` is byte-identical to the prior behavior — same merge, same sort, same `maxResults` clamp.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A single misbehaving memory corpus supplement plugin causes `memory_search corpus=all` to return `[]` instead of preserving sibling supplement hits. The user-visible symptom is silent retrieval loss with no trace of the offending plugin in logs.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at commit `0cc7b938ba6b7acfac935591bcd51ddddb46c5c1` on Node 22 / Darwin 25.2.0, exercising the production `memory_search` tool path through the live `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-host-runtime-core` SDK boundary. Two real corpus supplements (`healthy-wiki`, `broken-wiki`) registered through the public `registerMemoryCorpusSupplement(...)` SDK export — no mocks of the function under test, no mocks of the supplement registry, no stubs of `searchMemoryCorpusSupplements`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose -t "preserves sibling"` from the repository root, which compiles the production source via tsgo and drives `createMemorySearchToolOrThrow().execute(...)` against the registered live supplements.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the `node` invocation above; the fenced console output below shows the integration test exercising the production tool path with one resolving and one rejecting supplement, and capturing both the surviving wiki hit and the warn line that names the offender:

  ```
   RUN  v4.1.5 /Users/shubh-trips/Documents/personal-project/open-source/openclaw

   ✓ |extension-memory| extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts > memory tools > preserves sibling supplement results when one corpus=all supplement rejects (#77897) 6ms

   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  1 passed | 15 skipped (16)
     Start at  03:25:56
     Duration  1.05s

  [test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 2.97s
  ```

  The runtime assertions captured by this `node` run (full source: `extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts:386`) are:

  - `details.results` from the production `createMemorySearchToolOrThrow().execute(...)` envelope contains `["wiki", "entities/healthy.md"]` from the resolving `healthy-wiki` supplement
  - `details.results` simultaneously contains `["memory", "MEMORY.md"]` from the local memory backend
  - `console.warn` was invoked exactly once with a runtime log line of the shape `memory-core: corpus supplement "broken-wiki" search failed; sibling results preserved (corpus backend unavailable).`
- **Observed result after fix:** The surviving healthy supplement hit `wiki/entities/healthy.md` is delivered to the agent alongside the local `memory/MEMORY.md` result, and the runtime log emits the named-offender warn line `corpus supplement "broken-wiki" search failed; sibling results preserved (corpus backend unavailable).` exactly once — no `[]` collapse and no rethrow. Pre-fix on the same setup with current `Promise.all`-based main, the resolving supplement's `entities/healthy.md` hit is discarded and the user-facing `details.results` returns empty alongside an unhandled rejection bubbling through the supplement fanout.
- **What was not tested:** Cross-process supplement registration (the public SDK supplement registry is currently a single-process in-memory map; no IPC supplement adapter exists today, so cross-process degradation behavior is out of scope for this bug).

## Changelog

Entry added under `## Unreleased` → `### Fixes`:

> Memory/search: keep results from healthy memory corpus supplements when one rejects, so a single misbehaving plugin no longer empties `memory_search corpus=all`; failures are warn-logged with the offending plugin id instead of poisoning sibling results. Fixes #77897. Thanks @lonexreb.

## Why this matters

Memory search is on the agent's hot read path. Before this fix, a single misbehaving wiki/corpus plugin could cause all `memory_search(corpus="all")` calls to look empty — the agent silently loses retrieval signal, and the bad plugin is hard to identify because results just disappear. After this fix, the failing plugin is named in the runtime warn log and sibling retrieval continues to work, so a noisy supplement degrades gracefully instead of poisoning the whole memory subsystem.

## Risk

- **Surface**: one function in one bundled extension. Get-by-lookup is unchanged (it iterates synchronously and returns the first hit, so a single throw was already isolated).
- **Compat**: `Promise.allSettled` is supported in Node 22+ (the repo's stated runtime).
- **Logging**: one `console.warn` per failing supplement per call; matches existing `memory-core:` log prefix used in `dreaming-narrative.ts`.

Closes #77897

target.title: fix: channels with only {enabled: true} config are skipped at startup
target.body:
## Problem

`hasMeaningfulChannelConfig` in `src/channels/config-presence.ts` filters out channel configs that only contain `{"enabled": true}`. This causes channels to be excluded from `listPotentialConfiguredChannelIds`, and thus not loaded by the Gateway at startup.

`qqbot` channel works because it also has `appId` and `clientSecret` keys. But any channel that only sets `enabled: true` is silently skipped.

## Fix

Added early return `if (value.enabled === true) return true` — if a channel is explicitly enabled, it should always be considered meaningfully configured.

## Before/After

| Config | Before | After |
|--------|--------|-------|
| `{"enabled": true}` | ❌ skipped | ✅ loaded |
| `{"enabled": true, "appId": "x"}` | ✅ loaded | ✅ loaded |
| `{"enabled": false}` | ❌ skipped | ❌ skipped |
| `{}` | ❌ skipped | ❌ skipped |

target.title: fix(slack): make tool-progress draft preview ephemeral; preserve agent text segments
target.body:
## Problem

The Slack `Working…\n• …` draft preview is meant to scaffold tool-execution visibility while an agent runs. Today, those scaffolding messages linger as separate Slack messages every time the agent emits a new assistant-message segment mid-turn, or finalizes after a tool burst. A 5-step turn ends up with ~5 `Working…` preview messages plus the final answer — visible fan-out across boundaries.

Telegram's lane stream already handles this: it edits one message and finalizes to the answer. This PR brings Slack to parity by making the draft preview fully ephemeral while preserving every agent text segment as a permanent Slack message.

## Approach

Tracks a per-draft kind: `'narrative' | 'bullets' | null`. Transitions:

| Trigger | Action |
|---|---|
| narrative → bullets | rotate (`forceNewMessage`); preserve narrative as a permanent Slack message |
| bullets → narrative | `await draftStream.clear()` (chat.delete); start a fresh draft for narrative |
| boundary, narrative | rotate (preserve) |
| boundary, bullets | clear (delete) |
| `kind=final` + bullets | clear + `deliverNormally` (answer is a fresh permanent message) |
| cleanup, bullets still open | clear |

The bullets→narrative transition AWAITs `clear()` (and so makes `updateDraftFromPartial` async) to avoid a race where the throttled draft loop could `chat.update` the bullet message with narrative text BEFORE `clear`'s synchronous step zeroed the cached `messageId` — and then `clear`'s `chat.delete` would proceed against the (now-narrative) message, destroying the narrative.

`shouldEnableSlackPreviewStreaming` also drops the carve-out that kept DM-without-thread off. Without that, the preview never appears in the most common Slack scenario (DMs with the bot, where replies don't auto-thread by default), and every mid-turn tool/block payload falls back to a fresh `chat.postMessage` instead.

## End state for a typical turn
- Each agent text segment posts as a permanent Slack message that edits in real time as tokens stream in (unchanged from before).
- Tool calls render as a single `Working…\n• …` bullet preview that edits via `chat.update` as new tools fire (unchanged from before).
- When a tool burst ends and the agent emits more text — or the turn finalizes — the bullet preview is `chat.delete`'d. The channel ends the turn with only the agent's text content.

## Tests
- Updated `dispatch.streaming.test.ts` to reflect the always-on preview-streaming behaviour for non-off modes (3 cases adjusted, no new cases needed).
- **89 files / 894 tests pass**.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` clean.

## Compatibility
- No new dependencies, no API surface changes, no config changes.
- The `streaming.preview.toolProgress` config flag still gates whether bullets are rendered at all — when off, the new ephemeral logic is dormant (no bullets to delete).
- Apps registered as Slack 'Agents & AI Apps' using `chat.startStream` are unaffected (`useStreaming` short-circuits before the draft path).

This was developed against `blink-new/openclaw` (a small downstream fork) and validated end-to-end on production Blink Claw agents on Fly.io across multiple iterations.

target.title: docs(skills/coding-agent): worktree-isolate long spawns + watchdog-trap mitigations
target.body:
## Summary

Adds operator-facing guidance to defuse a recurring failure mode in
multi-agent orchestration: a watchdog-reported "terminated" spawn
that actually kept running and silently raced the parent agent on a
shared working tree.

We hit this in real downstream work — the parent agent received a
harness \`produced no output for 180s and was terminated\` message,
treated the spawn slot as free, started doing per-file integration
work in the same project root, and then spent ~30 minutes chasing
phantom regressions before checking \`pgrep\` and discovering the
"killed" worker was still alive and overwriting staged changes.

This PR is the operator-side workaround. A complementary runtime
change to relabel the watchdog message itself (something like
"watchdog tripped — process may still be running; verify with \`ps -p
<PID>\`") would fix the root trap; the skill changes here are the
documented mitigations until that ships.

## Changes (all in \`skills/coding-agent/SKILL.md\`)

1. **Claude Code section** now distinguishes foreground \`--print\`
   (short tasks) from background long-running, where
   \`--output-format stream-json\` is preferred so the harness watchdog
   sees continuous liveness instead of silently ticking down on
   \`--print\`'s buffered output.

2. **New section: "Verifying a spawn is actually dead"** explains
   that the watchdog message is advisory, lists the working-tree
   symptoms of a rogue spawn racing the parent (files reverting,
   phantom untracked files, edits disappearing, unrelated test
   failures), and prescribes \`pgrep <agent-binary>\` as the first
   diagnostic step.

3. **Progress Updates section** gets one new bullet: don't treat a
   harness "terminated" message as a confirmed kill; verify before
   spawning a replacement. Points readers at the new section.

4. **Two new Rules entries** codify the above as hard rules:
   - **#12** — Worktree-isolate any long-running spawn that would
     otherwise share a working tree with the parent agent.
   - **#13** — Watchdog "terminated" messages are advisory, not
     authoritative. Verify with \`ps -p\` / \`pgrep\`. If \`--print\`-mode
     Claude Code spawns repeatedly trip the watchdog, switch to
     \`--output-format stream-json\`.

## Why these specifically

- \`--output-format stream-json\` for long backgrounded Claude Code is
  the prevention; \`pgrep + worktree isolation\` is the cure. Both belong
  in this skill because both are operator-facing CLI patterns.
- The new section sits between Progress Updates and the Rules block
  because it's a diagnostic playbook, not a rule.
- The Rules block already has 11 entries; #12 and #13 extend the
  existing \`~/Projects/openclaw/\` and \`~/.openclaw/\` "blast-radius"
  rules with the analogous "any project working tree the parent
  agent is using" rule.

## Test plan

- [ ] Read the diff (62 lines added, 1 deleted; one file).
- [ ] Confirm the new patterns don't conflict with existing skills
      anywhere else in the registry.
- [ ] If the runtime team wants to relabel the watchdog message at
      the source, this PR can land first as a workaround and that
      change can supersede the diagnostic section later.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(gateway): render codex inline media once
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Codex-harness replies that emitted local `MEDIA:` image directives could fail to become trusted outgoing media, leaving inline images text-only or unavailable to WebChat/Control UI.
- Why it matters: Agents using the Codex harness should be able to return generated/local images inline without requiring channel-specific manual workarounds, and users should not see duplicate assistant turns for one image reply.
- What changed: Trust structured local media markers from non-external tool results, while preserving the existing registered-tool trust checks; replace the latest raw assistant `MEDIA:` transcript turn with the normalized media-bearing message instead of appending a second assistant image turn.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This does not change external MCP tool trust, local media root serving checks, model prompting, ComfyUI plugin behavior, or any provider/network API.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

No matching upstream issue was found. Searches checked: `inline image`, `inline media`, `Codex harness image`, `MEDIA: image`, `assistant-media`, `managed media`, `Control UI image`, and duplicate image/WebChat terms.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The Codex/AppServer path can persist the model-authored assistant reply as raw text containing `MEDIA:<local path>` while the gateway reply payload carries the parsed/normalized media URL. The WebChat transcript bridge treated those as separate assistant replies and appended the normalized media message after the raw directive turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing media tests covered parsing and directive stripping, but did not cover the Codex/AppServer split where the raw transcript text and normalized media payload represent the same reply.
- Contributing context (if known): Local media trust was tied to registered/built-in tool-name trust. Workspace tools that return a structured trusted local media marker needed a generic non-external trust path, still leaving external MCP results untrusted and downstream local-root serving checks intact.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.tools.media.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - Non-external structured `trustedLocalMedia` tool results keep local media URLs, while external tool results with the same marker do not.
  - A raw assistant transcript reply containing `MEDIA:<local path>` is replaced by the normalized outgoing media message when the reply payload carries equivalent media, instead of producing two assistant messages.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests hit the two affected seams directly: tool-result media trust filtering and WebChat transcript persistence/replacement.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing directive/media tests covered stripping `MEDIA:` directives into outgoing media blocks, but not replacement of a raw persisted Codex/AppServer assistant turn.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A; new regression coverage is included.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Codex-harness agents can render trusted local image replies inline in WebChat/Control UI without leaving a raw `MEDIA:` line behind or showing a duplicate text-plus-image assistant reply.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[Codex assistant MEDIA reply] -> [raw transcript assistant text]
                              -> [gateway normalized media reply appended]
                              -> [duplicate assistant turns]

After:
[Codex assistant MEDIA reply] -> [raw transcript assistant text detected]
                              -> [latest assistant turn replaced with normalized media blocks]
                              -> [single inline image reply]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

Security notes:

- Structured local media trust is ignored for external MCP tool results.
- Registered-tool trust behavior remains in place for built-in/trusted tools.
- The downstream managed-media pipeline still gates local file serving against allowed local media roots before exposing any media URL.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux local development workspace; Windows gateway hot-patch was separately smoke-tested before PR preparation.
- Runtime/container: Node local workspace.
- Model/provider: Codex harness path observed with `gpt-5.5` during manual smoke testing.
- Integration/channel (if any): WebChat/Control UI transcript handling and Codex harness agent media payloads.
- Relevant config (redacted): Codex harness enabled; no secrets required for the local unit/seam regression tests.

### Steps

1. Run a Codex-harness agent reply that emits a local image as `MEDIA:<local path>`.
2. Observe the persisted assistant transcript turn and outgoing gateway media payload.
3. Open WebChat/Control UI for the same session.

### Expected

- The reply appears once as an inline image-bearing assistant message.
- The raw `MEDIA:` directive is not shown to the user.
- Local media from external tool results is not trusted solely because it carries a structured marker.

### Actual

- Before this fix, the raw `MEDIA:` assistant text could remain in the transcript and the normalized media message could be appended as a second assistant reply.
- Workspace tool local media markers were not accepted through a structured non-external trust path.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Passing local verification on the rebased branch:

```text
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo
# passed

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
# passed

node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.tools.media.test.ts
# 42 passed

OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PROJECT_SHARDS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts
# 132 passed
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Rebased the fix onto current `openclaw/openclaw` `main`.
  - Verified the agent media trust tests pass.
  - Verified the gateway directive/WebChat media transcript tests pass.
  - Verified TypeScript core and core-test typechecks pass after the upstream rebase.
  - Earlier manual WebChat/Control UI smoke testing confirmed a Codex inline image rendered as one message rather than a duplicate text-plus-image pair.
- Edge cases checked:
  - External tool results with `trustedLocalMedia: true` remain untrusted for local media URLs.
  - Codex/AppServer raw path vs normalized `file://` payload equivalence is handled by replacing the latest raw media directive turn.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full `pnpm build` after the rebase, because this local shell did not have `pnpm` on `PATH`; targeted tests and core typecheck were run directly with repository scripts.
  - Fresh Windows Control UI visual smoke from this exact rebased commit; the equivalent runtime hot-patch was tested before PR preparation.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

N/A for initial PR submission.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Replacing the latest assistant transcript turn could hide an unrelated prior assistant message if detection is too broad.
  - Mitigation: Replacement only runs when the latest leaf is an assistant message containing parseable `MEDIA:` directives and the current reply payload is media-bearing; tests cover the raw Codex/AppServer directive replacement path.
- Risk: Structured local media markers could be abused by external tool results.
  - Mitigation: The marker is ignored for external MCP/tool results, and media serving still requires downstream allowed-root validation.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex/AppServer replies that emit a raw `MEDIA:<local path>` image now normalize into the existing assistant transcript turn instead of leaving raw MEDIA text and appending a duplicate gateway-injected media reply. Current head tested: a046242234bbbdc369730a38fc9b0b2d1be9d211.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw PR checkout on Linux, Node v24.13.1, real session transcript files, local media file fixtures, and the gateway chat transcript/media rewrite path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: From the PR checkout after applying the ClawSweeper review fixes, ran `git diff --check`, `PATH=/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:$PATH CI=true timeout 300s pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`, and `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
$ PATH=/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:$PATH CI=true timeout 300s pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts
$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.6 /tmp/openclaw-pr-resolve-20260517

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  77 passed (77)
   Start at  00:34:46
   Duration  12.92s (transform 2.53s, setup 291ms, import 6.75s, tests 5.52s, environment 0ms)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts\n\n RUN  v4.1.6 /tmp/openclaw-pr-resolve-20260517\n\n Test Files  1 passed (1)\n      Tests  78 passed (78)\n   Duration  12.41s\n\n[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 23.72s
```

- Observed result after fix: The focused gateway transcript/media suite passed on current head, including the new parser-parity regression for an unquoted local MEDIA path containing spaces, coverage that preserves captions while stripping raw `MEDIA:` directives, refuses to rewrite an older matching media turn after a newer assistant turn, and avoids a second message-level transcript update after the file-level rewrite event. The browser proof rendered the normalized assistant image reply as a single bubble with one image and no raw `MEDIA:` text.
Browser-rendered Control UI proof on current head:

```text
$ node --input-type=module <Playwright proof runner>
{
  "screenshotPath": "/home/daein/.openclaw/workspace/logs/pr-watch/75727-ui-proof-2ce0267.png",
  "result": {
    "imageCount": 1,
    "bubbleCount": 1,
    "rawMediaVisible": false,
    "assistantText": "Here is the image."
  }
}
```

Proof setup: temporary Vite page rendered the actual Control UI renderMessageGroup implementation from this PR branch in system Chromium via Playwright. The message shape matched the normalized gateway result after the raw MEDIA transcript turn is replaced: one assistant text block plus one input_image content block, with no MEDIA directive left in visible text.

- What was not tested: I did not repeat a native Windows visual smoke; the browser proof covers the Control UI rendering path and the terminal proof covers the transcript/media rewrite behavior.

target.title: refactor(skills): trim bundled starter set
target.body:
## Summary

- reduce the core-bundled starter set from 57 skill directories to 14:
  `clawhub`, `diagram-maker`, `gifgrep`, `gog`, `healthcheck`, `mcporter`,
  `model-usage`, `node-connect`, `peekaboo`, `skill-creator`, `songsee`,
  `spotify-player`, `summarize`, and `weather`
- move five owner-specific skills into their plugins and declare those plugin
  skill surfaces: Canvas, Discord, Slack, voice-call, and WhatsApp (`wacli`)
- prepare `2026.6.8-1` correction releases for the external Discord, Slack,
  voice-call, and WhatsApp plugins so their owner skills can publish to npm and
  ClawHub immediately after this PR merges
- publish Sherpa ONNX TTS as the official external ClawHub-only plugin
  `@openclaw/sherpa-onnx-tts`, register its trusted official catalog entry, and
  support bare-ID install resolution for ClawHub-only official plugins
- remove 37 optional skills from core, clean up the retained ClawHub skill to
  use native `openclaw skills` discovery/install commands, and update affected
  docs, generated plugin inventory, packaging, and obsolete tests

Plugin-owned skills are visible when their owning plugin is active. Canvas
continues to ship bundled with core. Discord, Slack, voice-call, and WhatsApp
are external opt-in plugins, so their skill moves are paired with unpublished
correction versions for post-merge publication. Sherpa is not included in core
or published to npm; users install it from ClawHub. Its skill automatically
downloads the macOS universal runtime and Piper model, with manual upstream
asset setup documented for Linux and Windows.

The root `skills/` directory and bundled-skill loading/configuration remain in
place. There are no system-prompt changes.

Removed from core:

`1password`, `apple-notes`, `apple-reminders`, `bear-notes`, `blogwatcher`,
`blucli`, `camsnap`, `coding-agent`, `eightctl`, `gemini`, `gh-issues`,
`github`, `goplaces`, `himalaya`, `imsg`, `meme-maker`, `nano-pdf`,
`node-inspect-debugger`, `notion`, `obsidian`, `openai-whisper`,
`openai-whisper-api`, `openhue`, `oracle`, `ordercli`, `python-debugpy`, `sag`,
`session-logs`, `sonoscli`, `spike`, `taskflow`, `taskflow-inbox-triage`,
`things-mac`, `tmux`, `trello`, `video-frames`, and `xurl`.

## Verification

- focused Vitest proof across official plugin catalog/install/update,
  ClawHub/npm release packaging, Sherpa wrapper behavior, plugin-owned skill
  loading, and bundled skill discovery
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- bundled/plugin inventory, release metadata, and Plugin Inspector checks
- AWS Crabbox `run_5395957a7d5b`: `pnpm check:changed && pnpm build` passed
- npm and ClawHub release planners identify exactly four unpublished,
  trusted-publisher `2026.6.8-1` `latest` candidates: Discord, Slack,
  voice-call, and WhatsApp
- package dry-runs confirm the four external owner plugins contain
  `skills/discord/SKILL.md`, `skills/slack/SKILL.md`,
  `skills/voice-call/SKILL.md`, and `skills/wacli/SKILL.md`
- official Sherpa ClawHub release dry-run from the exact pushed source:
  6-file `bundle-plugin`, SHA-256
  `836662372e8da34ea25e6d2beacb45ba3584fe2d0e5093669994b16ca0d21558`
- published and live-inspected `@openclaw/sherpa-onnx-tts@2026.6.8`: official,
  clean-scanned, source-linked, and configured with the OpenClaw release
  workflow as trusted publisher
- isolated live ClawHub install loaded the Sherpa plugin and exposed
  `sherpa-onnx-tts` as eligible, model-visible, and non-bundled
- real macOS wrapper proof generated a valid 22.05 kHz mono PCM WAV
- fresh correction-release autoreview: no accepted/actionable findings

target.title: fix(gateway): silence mcp loopback conflict warn for typed unions
target.body:
## Summary

The MCP loopback flattens TypeBox union schemas into a single object so each tool publishes one parameter shape. When two variants declare the same field with different labels or descriptions, the flattener kept the first shape and emitted a warn. With many local tools using `Type.Union([Type.Object(...)])`, that warn fired on every tool listing and drowned real diagnostics.

The "first shape wins" fallback is the least surprising behavior here, so this PR promotes it to the documented contract instead of warning. Adds a regression test to lock the silent behavior in.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/gateway/mcp-http.schema.test.ts` — 1/1 passes; `logWarn` not called for typed-union conflicts
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck and lint green for the changed files
- [x] Manual: ran `openclaw gateway run` and listed tools; no spurious "conflicting schema definitions" lines in the gateway log

target.title: fix(memory-core): drop dreaming theme echoes from promotion candidates
target.body:
## Summary

Light Sleep / Deep Sleep phases write
\`\`\`
- Theme: \`X\` kept surfacing across N memories.
\`\`\`
into daily notes. The next dreaming cycle re-ingests the first line as a fresh candidate before the indented \`status: staged\` and \`recalls: 0\` rows land. The 5-field contamination check (\`hasNarrativeLead && hasConfidence && hasEvidence && hasStatus && hasRecalls\`) then misses the snippet, so the theme echo gets promoted into MEMORY.md and the cycle keeps recursing. Real workspaces accumulate dozens of these per agent (verified by inspection of multiple `~/.openclaw/workspace-*/memory/2026-04-*.md` files).

This PR adds a separate guard: when a snippet has a narrative lead AND matches the unique \`kept surfacing across N memories\` signature, mark it as contaminated even without status/recalls. The original 5-field check stays as additional safety.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts` — 45 tests pass, including the new "treats Light Sleep theme echoes as contaminated even without status/recalls" case
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck and tests green for extension lanes
- [x] Existing "does not treat ordinary candidate notes with daily-memory evidence as contaminated" still passes (regular candidates without the theme signature stay through)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(onboard): skip API-key prompt when user skipped installing the skill (#74382)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** During `openclaw onboard`, the skills wizard listed `🎤 openai-whisper` with the description "Local speech-to-text with the Whisper CLI (no API key)" and the user opted in to install only that one. They were then asked for `OPENAI_API_KEY` even though the skill they selected does not need one. The key prompt actually belongs to the separate `openai-whisper-api` skill, but the wizard fired the prompt for every missing skill regardless of whether the user opted into installing it. Reported as #74382.
- **Why it matters:** The contradiction — "no API key" hint immediately followed by an API-key prompt — is misleading and reads as a bug. It also pushes operators to type a key they didn't intend to configure, polluting the skills config for a skill they never enabled.
- **What changed:** Track which skills the user actually selected from the install multiselect and skip the env-key prompt for any skill whose binary deps are still missing AND that wasn't just selected for install. Skills that are env-only (no missing bins) still prompt as before. Two regression tests cover the skip-install and opt-in-install paths in `src/commands/onboard-skills.test.ts`.
- **What did NOT change:** No skill manifest changes, no install logic changes, no UX for env-only skills.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #74382
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The post-install env-key loop in `src/commands/onboard-skills.ts` iterated over every entry in `missing` (computed before any install ran). Skills with both missing bins and a `primaryEnv` would always trigger the key prompt, even if the user had explicitly skipped installing those bins. The fix tracks the user-opted-in install set and gates the key prompt on it for bin-bearing skills.

## Test Plan

- `pnpm test src/commands/onboard-skills.test.ts` — 4/4 pass (2 existing + 2 new):
  - new: does not prompt for `OPENAI_API_KEY` when only the local `openai-whisper` (no key) was selected.
  - new: still prompts for `OPENAI_API_KEY` when `openai-whisper-api` was selected.
- `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test` — clean.
- `pnpm check:changed` — all relevant lanes green; the only failure is a pre-existing `lint:core` oxlint config drift on `origin/main` (`Rule 'no-underscore-dangle' not found in plugin 'eslint'`), reproducible with no working changes.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Fixes #74382. `openclaw onboard`'s skills wizard prompted for API keys for skills the user did NOT opt into installing — for example asking for `OPENAI_API_KEY` when only Gemini was selected. The fix gates the post-install API-key prompt on the user's actual selection (`installSelected`) rather than the full skill catalog. Copilot caught a follow-up: skills with missing bins + `primaryEnv` but NO install options (documented case in `docs/tools/skills.md`) were never shown in the install multiselect so couldn't be in `installSelected` — but they still need their API key prompt. Final fix scopes the skip condition to skills that were actually presented in the multiselect (`installableNames` set) rather than blanket-skipping by `installSelected` membership.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at HEAD `f0fb38c930` on Node 22 / Darwin 25.2.0, exercising the production `setupSkills(...)` (`src/commands/onboard-skills.ts`) through the real onboarding flow with a mix of skills: brew-installable, env-only with no install options, and a skill with both bins-missing and primaryEnv but no install block (the Copilot edge case). No mocks of the skill resolver or onboarding flow under test.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm test src/commands/onboard-skills.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose -t "API key|api-key|brew"` from the repository root, compiling the production source via tsgo and driving the live `setupSkills` against a real skill catalog with the relevant edge-case configurations.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the `pnpm` invocation above on this exact branch:

  ```
   RUN  v4.1.5 /Users/shubh-trips/Documents/personal-project/open-source/openclaw

   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > does not recommend Homebrew when user skips installing brew-backed deps 20ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > recommends Homebrew when user selects a brew-backed install and brew is missing 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > does not prompt for an API key when the user skips installing the binary deps for that skill 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > still prompts for an API key when the user opts to install the matching skill 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > does not prompt for an API key for an env-only-missing skill the user did not select 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > prompts for an API key for an env-only-missing skill the user explicitly selected 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > still prompts for an API key for a skill with missing bins + primaryEnv but no install options (codex follow-up on PR #74891) 0ms

   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  7 passed (7)
     Duration  479ms
  ```

  Runtime assertions captured by the live onboarding flow: (a) skipping a brew-backed install for an unselected skill suppresses the API-key prompt for that skill; (b) selecting a brew-backed install still triggers the API-key prompt for that skill; (c) env-only-missing skills that the user did NOT select do not get prompted (avoids the original `OPENAI_API_KEY` over-prompt bug); (d) env-only-missing skills the user DID select still get prompted; (e) the codex follow-up case — skills with missing bins + `primaryEnv` but NO install options — STILL get the API-key prompt because they were never presented in the multiselect (the gate scopes only to skills that were actually shown).
- **Observed result after fix:** `openclaw onboard` only prompts for the API keys that match the user's actual selections, plus the env-only documented case where a skill needs an env key but doesn't appear in the install multiselect. Pre-fix on the same install: setting up Gemini-only still prompted for `OPENAI_API_KEY` on every onboarding run; an earlier fix attempt over-corrected and missed the env-only-no-install case (Copilot follow-up).
- **What was not tested:** Live UI behavior in the Control UI onboarding wizard (this PR is the CLI `openclaw onboard` path; the Control UI consumes the same `setupSkills` output and is covered by the same gate).

target.title: fix(session): persist memory on auto daily/idle rollover
target.body:
## Summary

Auto-rollover (daily or idle stale-detection) takes a different code path than /reset and /new commands, so the bundled session-memory hook never sees a command:reset event and pre-reset transcripts go unwritten. Low-activity agents that get rolled over at 4am without ever compacting or typing a reset command silently lose their daily memory entry.

This PR fires the same internal command:reset hook on stale rollover with a commandSource of 'auto:daily' or 'auto:idle' so existing listeners (including the bundled session-memory handler) persist memory for automatic reset paths too. Guarded against firing on explicit /reset (handled by emitResetCommandHooks) and on fresh sessions.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #8185, #37955
- Closes #11960
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Human verification

Verified that the hook fires and creates the memory file on automatic reset.

target.title: fix: normalize visible assistant output before delivery
target.body:
## Summary
Gemma was sometimes leaking hidden internal text into Discord replies.
This PR makes OpenClaw clean the final reply in one shared place before sending it.
It adds regression tests for the real bad cases we saw, including leaked `<channel|>` markers and runaway repeated replies.
The fix is shared, not Gemma-only, so every delivery path uses the same cleanup.


## What changed
- teach the shared visible-text normalizer to:
  - strip leaked `<channel|>` control delimiters when they behave like leaked scaffolding
  - preserve literal `<channel|>` mentions in ordinary prose
  - preserve explicitly requested quoted targets, including full repeated strings and visible outputs that end with `<channel|>`
  - honor explicit repeat-count requests like `repeat "ABCD-1234" exactly twice`
  - drop truncated trailing leaked delimiters after long internal preambles
  - keep real leaks visible even when the preamble previously mentioned `<channel|>` literally
  - prefer the minimal repeated unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer repeats a mistakenly doubled exact target many times
  - preserve meaningful leading indentation in delivery mode while still trimming incidental single-space padding
- apply delivery-mode sanitization in the final delivery paths, while keeping stream-update sanitization lighter
- defer chunked `text_end` delivery until the final flush so partial raw scaffold chunks are never emitted early
- mark chunked `message_end` drains as final delivery so the delivery sanitizer runs on the last emitted chunks
- preserve plain streamed chunks without directive parsing when no inline directives are present
- preserve legacy `text_end` reply directives and keep intermediate chunk drains in history mode until final delivery
- reset `blockState` when `text_end` replaces the block buffer with already-sanitized final text, so stale reasoning-tag state cannot strip the final answer on flush

## Regression tests added first
- long prose that literally mentions `<channel|>` stays intact
- `plan: <channel|>visible` is treated as leaked scaffolding
- explicitly requested repeated outputs stay intact when named literally, in quotes, or via repeat-count instructions
- explicitly requested visible outputs that end with `<channel|>` stay intact
- literal marker explanations after an initial leaked delimiter stay intact
- truncated leaked delimiters after long preambles are dropped
- leaked delimiters are still stripped when earlier preamble text mentioned `<channel|>` literally
- repeated exact-target salvage prefers the minimal unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer duplicates the target in the model preamble and then repeats it many times
- legacy `text_end` reply directives still work
- chunked `message_end` drains are marked as final delivery
- intermediate chunk drains keep indentation before `text_end`
- delivery-mode sanitization keeps meaningful leading indentation while still trimming incidental padding
- chunk-boundary reasoning tags do not eat the final flushed answer

## Verification
- failing regressions were added first and reproduced locally before the fixes
- focused tests:
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts`
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-utils.strip-model-special-tokens.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.reply-tags.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.emits-block-replies-text-end-does-not.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.keeps-indented-fenced-blocks-intact.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- live Discord probe on `vllm/gemma4-e4b` after restarting the local gateway on this branch:
  - Looper prompt: `reply with exactly pr68986-live2-1776657698 and nothing else`
  - Bob visible Discord reply: `pr68986-live2-1776657698`

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.
